cataloging_subject.label: King Horn
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The anonymous Middle English romance King Horn as an abstract work represented by three substantially variant complete manuscript texts. This subject excludes the individual witnesses, Joseph Hall's 1901 parallel-text edition, U-M's OCR encoding, and the repository XML. It also excludes Horn Childe and Maiden Rimnild, a distinct treatment of the Horn story that Hall prints as an appendix and U-M encodes in full.

catalog_summary.title: King Horn
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous
catalog_summary.date: Probably later thirteenth century; traditionally dated c. 1225, with the 1270s proposed as a plausible estimate
catalog_summary.region: Southern or southeastern England; exact place of composition disputed

identifiers.scheme: New Index of Middle English Verse
Digital Index of Middle English Verse

notes: The spelling 'Geste of Kyng Horn' appears as a witness title in the Harley catalog; the Cambridge digital catalog uses 'The Geste of King Horn'.

agents.name: Joseph Hall
notes: Editor of the 1901 Clarendon Press parallel-text edition.
The three surviving scribes and the modern editors are not authors of the anonymous cross-witness work.

scope.description: Separate close transcriptions of the Harley, Laud, and Cambridge texts
Parallel alignment, comparison, and manuscript-relationship analysis

date_statements.display: Probably the later thirteenth century; the older conventional date is c. 1225, while the 1270s have been proposed as a plausible estimate

assertion.notes: The normalized span follows PCMEP's broad evidential range; it does not imply composition throughout those years.

notes: The dates of the three surviving manuscripts are later and must not be substituted for the work's composition date.

places.label: Southeastern England outside Kent, near the Midland border

scope.description: Reconstructed language of the work, not any one scribal witness

notes: The witnesses have distinct scribal dialects and do not preserve one unchanged authorial dialect.

assertion.notes: Hall proposed northwestern Surrey; later work has found the phonology compatible with London, but neither location is secure.

scope.description: Selected narrative episodes involving Horn's second exile, service, combat, and alliance; no stable content-part structure is asserted

notes: This is a narrative setting, not evidence for the place of composition.

language_statements.label: Middle English
notes: The repository header's generic 'eng' code is superseded here by direct identification of the medieval language as Middle English. Modern English, French, Latin, and German occur only in editorial or scholarly layers.

form_statements.description: Short-line narrative verse in rhyming couplets, usually with three stresses and substantial alliterative patterning, though metre and lineation vary. Harley 2253 writes two printed verses on many manuscript long lines.

subject_statements.label: Christian-Saracen conflict and conversion
notes: The label describes the romance's medieval antagonistic religious framework; it is not a modern endorsement of that representation.

summaries.value: After Saracen invaders kill King Murry and seize Suddene, his son Horn and twelve companions are set adrift and reach Westernesse. Horn grows into a knight and pledges himself to Rymenhild, but the traitor Fikenhild falsely accuses him and causes a second exile. In Ireland Horn defeats a Saracen champion and gathers allies; he returns in disguise to prevent Rymenhild's forced marriage, reconquers Suddene, restores Christian rule and his inheritance, and finally rescues Rymenhild from Fikenhild. The romance joins love and recognition with exile, loyal companionship, betrayal, martial testing, and legitimate political restoration.

This record describes the anonymous medieval romance across its three surviving witnesses. Hall's edition, the U-M encoding, and the repository artifact are separate lineage layers. Horn Childe and Maiden Rimnild, although fully encoded after King Horn in the same XML, is a distinct related romance and is not a part of this work.

King Horn survives complete in British Library Harley MS 2253, Bodleian MS Laud Misc. 108, and Cambridge University Library MS Gg.4.27(2). Hall prints the three texts in parallel rather than constructing one unified eclectic poem; U-M serializes those parallel columns into three consecutive version divisions. No stable cross-witness internal part structure is imposed here.

extent.description: One continuous verse romance with no universal line count assigned. The repository encoding has 1,546 L elements for Harley 2253, 1,569 for Laud Misc. 108, and 1,530 for Gg.4.27(2). These are separate complete witness texts, not parts to be added; the XML's further 1,138 L elements belong to the distinct Horn Childe appendix and include two ellipsis placeholders for lacunae.

related_works.title: Horn et Rimenhild
notes: An Anglo-Norman treatment of the Horn story. Competing historical theories propose relationships among the versions, but Hall also records the judgment that no secure filiation is established; this record therefore uses only related_to.

related_works.title: Horn Childe and Maiden Rimnild
notes: A distinct, uniquely witnessed tail-rhyme romance in the Auchinleck Manuscript, printed by Hall as an appendix and fully retained by U-M. It is not a sequel or intrinsic part of King Horn.

related_works.title: Hind Horn
notes: A later ballad tradition sharing Horn-story material; no direct derivation from King Horn is asserted here.

evidence.locator: HEADER; three King Horn version divisions; Horn Childe appendix division
evidence.summary: Supplies the U-M identifier and source edition, the three separately encoded King Horn versions, the fully encoded but distinct Horn Childe appendix, language-header mismatch, and OCR/TEI production statement.

evidence.citation: Element, milestone, and division inspection of repository acn1637.xml, checked against Hall's page layout.
evidence.locator: DIV1, L, LG, MILESTONE, NOTE1, and PB elements
evidence.summary: Counts 1,546 Harley lines, 1,569 Laud lines, 1,530 Cambridge lines, and 1,138 Horn Childe L elements. The last total comprises 1,136 verse lines plus two ellipsis L placeholders. The XML has 396 PB instances but only 352 unique PB/@REF values because Hall's parallel Harley and Laud columns share 44 printed page images and are serialized into consecutive divisions.

evidence.citation: Joseph Hall, ed., King Horn: A Middle-English Romance, Edited from the Manuscripts (Oxford: Clarendon Press, 1901), lvi, 238 pp., Internet Archive scan.
evidence.locator: title page; introduction pp. vii-lvi; parallel texts pp. 1-89; Notes pp. 91-177; Horn Childe pp. 179-192
evidence.summary: Primary evidence for Hall's editor role, three-witness parallel method, transcription and correction policy, metre and story discussion, historical witness descriptions, the distinct status of Horn Childe, and the absence of secure filiation among Horn-story versions.

evidence.citation: Parsed Corpus of Middle English Poetry, Text Information: King Horn.
evidence.summary: Provides alternative title, plot, genre and subjects, debated 1200-1280 composition range with a suggested 1275 date, southern/southeastern localization discussion, couplet form, verse-index identifiers, three-witness census, and the 1,530-line Cambridge-based parsed text.

evidence.citation: Ronald B. Herzman, Graham Drake, and Eve Salisbury, eds., Introduction to King Horn, Middle English Texts Series, in Four Romances of England.
evidence.summary: Describes the poem as probably the oldest surviving Middle English romance, dates it to the later thirteenth century, identifies Matter of England and oral/folk connections, explains its rhymed and alliterative verse, and supports themes of exile, maturation, loyalty, betrayal, and political restoration.

evidence.citation: Database of Middle English Romance, King Horn.
evidence.summary: Supplies a detailed plot, later-thirteenth-century date, southern dialect with Midland forms, rhyming-couplet form, IMEV 166, thematic keywords, and current folio ranges for the three witnesses.

evidence.citation: Middle English Romances, Horn Childe & Maiden Rimnild.
evidence.summary: Identifies Horn Childe and Maiden Rimnild as a separate romance, assigns (N)IMEV 2253, describes its twelve-line aabaabccbddb tail-rhyme stanzas, and records the unique Auchinleck witness.

evidence.citation: ARLIMA: Archives de littérature du Moyen Âge, King Horn.
evidence.summary: Provides the established title, anonymous romance classification, older c. 1225 dating, Harley-based 1,546-line extent, incipit and explicit, three-witness census, and edition bibliography. Its single line total and early date are retained as attributed older conventions rather than universal facts.

evidence.citation: General Corpus lineage manifest for acn1637, current institutional witness catalogs and facsimiles.
evidence.summary: Binds the descriptive record to exact current records and folio ranges for Harley MS 2253, MS Laud Misc. 108, and MS Gg.4.27(2), while keeping their dates and scribal dialects at witness level.

evidence.citation: General Corpus lineage manifest for acn1637, Horn Childe appendix and Auchinleck witness research.
evidence.summary: Establishes Horn Childe and Maiden Rimnild as a distinct, uniquely witnessed 1,136-line tail-rhyme romance in Advocates MS 19.2.1, ending imperfect and printed as Hall's appendix with selected supplied readings.

open_questions.question: Can the broad later-thirteenth-century date and southern or southeastern localization of King Horn be refined without treating one witness's scribal dialect as the work's original language?
next_steps: Consult Rosamund Allen's 1984 edition and 1988 date-and-provenance study directly.
Compare current linguistic profiles for all three witnesses and distinguish rhyme evidence from scribal spelling.

open_questions.question: What, if any, direct textual relationship can current scholarship establish among King Horn, Horn et Rimenhild, Horn Childe and Maiden Rimnild, and the Hind Horn ballad tradition?
next_steps: Review modern comparative work after Hall, including Allen and Mills, before replacing related_to with a directional relation.
Keep shared story material distinct from evidence of direct textual copying.

open_questions.question: Is there a defensible canonical lineation or work-level internal structure that can be mapped across all three complete King Horn witnesses?
next_steps: Compare a modern synoptic edition and manuscript lineation, especially Harley's two-verses-per-written-line layout.
Until then retain witness-specific line counts and content_structure_status=summary.

notes: The XML header declares only 'eng'; direct inspection and scholarship establish Middle English as the work language.
The 5,783 L elements in the XML are not a work-level extent: they sum three complete variant texts of King Horn and the separate Horn Childe appendix.
Hall's manuscript genealogy and specific original-dialect localization are historical scholarly hypotheses, not unqualified facts.
Witness dates, localizations, layouts, and image rights remain attached to their exact manuscript or facsimile layers in the lineage manifest.
